narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical student's 3.21—and a solid LSAT score of 41, which beats the average of 36.70. You're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students in our dataset at over 92%. However, your earlier academic decile from Year 1 placed you in the 2nd decile, lower than the average 5.71, and especially your Year 3 decile of 1st—far below the typical 5.53—stood out as areas of concern. Coming from a lower-middle family income bracket also factored in, compared to higher averages across applicants. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 68.8% chance you'll face failure on that critical test. This high predicted risk is why your application was rejected—to protect the school's bar passage rates and your future career prospects. The biggest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your low Year 3 decile, signaling weaker performance in later undergrad years compared to peers who typically pass the bar. Your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success, much like it does for top performers. Your Year 1 decile added some additional push toward risk, while your family income had a modest negative influence relative to higher-earning backgrounds. Your GPA and full-time status offered slight positives but weren't enough to outweigh the top concerns. Compared to typical students who get approved, your later-year grades lag behind those in higher deciles who dominate bar passers. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted improvement or additional coursework, retake the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, and consider programs offering bar prep support tailored for diverse students like you—many succeed by addressing these exact gaps. You've already overcome so much; with strategic next steps, law school doors can reopen. We're rooting for you.
